edges.description: Synovial hyperplasia and pannus formation lead to progressive destruction of articular cartilage and bone.
Epigenetic dysregulation promotes autoreactive T cell programs that drive the autoimmune response
edges.source: Epigenetic Dysregulation of T Cell Function

edges.description: Distinct pathogenic fibroblast and macrophage subsets in the inflamed synovium drive cartilage and bone destruction via their respective inflammatory and matrix-degrading programs.

nodes.description: The immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own tissues, particularly the synovial membrane lining the joints.

evidence.reference_title: Autoimmune arthritis: the interface between the immune system and joints.
evidence.snippet: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is an autoimmune disease, characterized by chronic inflammation and synovial hyperplasia in the joints that ultimately lead to cartilage and bone destruction. A wealth of research has shown that CD4(+) T cells, especially IL-17 producing helper T (Th17) cells, play an important role in RA development.

evidence.reference_title: Th17 cells in renal inflammation and autoimmunity.
evidence.snippet: These pro-inflammatory T-cells are also key players in autoimmunity and a pathogenic role has been demonstrated in several diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis or psoriasis.

evidence.reference_title: The metabolic signature of T cells in rheumatoid arthritis.
evidence.snippet: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a prototypic autoimmune disease manifesting as chronic inflammation of the synovium and leading to acceleration of cardiovascular disease and shortening of life expectancy.

nodes.description: Chronic inflammation and pannus formation lead to the erosion of articular cartilage and underlying bone.

evidence.reference_title: Cellular and molecular pathways of structural damage in rheumatoid arthritis.
evidence.snippet: Structural damage of cartilage and bone tissue is a hallmark of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). The resulting joint destruction constitutes one of the major disease consequences for patients... These factors result in an inflammatory milieu in the affected joints which leads to an increased development and function of osteoclasts.

Chronic inflammatory arthritis not only leads to inflammatory bone loss but it also involves local erosion of articular bone. This osteo-destructive feature of chronic inflammatory arthritis is a major cause of disability in patients with rheumatoid arthritis.
Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic inflammatory disease characterised by joint swelling, joint tenderness and destruction of synovial joints, leading to severe disability.

evidence.reference_title: Effects of targeted therapies on bone in rheumatic and musculoskeletal diseases.
evidence.snippet: Rheumatoid arthritis can involve localized, periarticular bone erosion...The RANK-RANKL-osteoprotegerin axis and the Wnt-beta-catenin signalling pathway...have been implicated in inflammatory bone loss.

nodes.description: Aberrant chromatin remodeling and histone modifications alter T cell gene expression programs, promoting autoreactive T cell activation and loss of immune tolerance. Changes in DNA methylation and histone acetylation at immune gene loci contribute to disease susceptibility and chronicity.
nodes.id: Epigenetic Dysregulation of T Cell Function

evidence.reference_title: Spectrum of Joint Deformities in Children with Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis.
evidence.snippet: More than 50% patients with JIA have joint deformities. Joint deformities are more likely to be seen in children with long-standing disease, those with polyarthritis JIA and seropositive patients.

evidence.reference_title: Initial presentation of early rheumatoid arthritis.
evidence.snippet: Incident RA presents mainly as symmetric arthritis.

evidence.reference_title: Patterns of Involvement of the Hand Joints in Classical Rheumatoid Arthritis.
evidence.snippet: Symmetrical involvement of the hand joints is described as characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis (RA).

evidence.reference_title: Rheumatoid arthritis of the cervical spine--clinical considerations.
evidence.snippet: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic, systemic inflammatory disorder affecting multiple organ systems, joints, ligaments, and bones and commonly involves the cervical spine.

evidence.reference_title: What is the natural history of rheumatoid arthritis?
evidence.snippet: RA is now thought to be associated with a conserved sequence of amino acids in a number of HLA-DRB1 alleles, called the RA shared epitope

nodes.description: Single-cell and mass cytometry profiling reveals discrete synovial fibroblast and macrophage subsets with distinct inflammatory and destructive potential. Disease-specific cellular phenotypes enable stratification into responder and non-responder groups for targeted therapy. Integration of synovial multi-omics signatures with clinical data supports precision medicine approaches.

evidence.snippet: Cellular and molecular deconvolution of synovial tissue heterogeneity reveals discrete disease phenotypes, enabling disease stratification and response prediction. Recent advances in single-cell RNA sequencing and mass cytometry have defined more refined synovial molecular signatures and cell-type abundance phenotypes, uncovering novel pathogenic cellular subsets and inflammatory crosstalk networks.

Despite the success of targeted therapies in rheumatoid arthritis, the lack of predictive biomarkers of response leads to an empirical treatment approach, often delaying effective intervention due to non-response to the initially selected individualized medication regimens in approximately 40% of patients.

nodes.description: The synovial membrane becomes thickened and inflamed, leading to the formation of pannus tissue that invades cartilage and bone.

evidence.snippet: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic inflammatory disease with progressive articular damage. Activated cells of the synovium produce pro-inflammatory and matrix-degrading effector molecules, which maintain the inflammation and lead to the destruction of the involved joints. In addition to macrophages and T- and B-cells, fibroblast-like synoviocytes must be considered key cells in driving the pathological processes. They can be distinguished by their transformed-appearing phenotype and their invasion into adjacent cartilage and bone.

evidence.reference_title: Synovial Macrophages: Past Life, Current Situation, and Application in Inflammatory Arthritis.
evidence.snippet: Synovial hyperplasia often presents when joints become inflamed due to immune cell infiltration. Synovial membrane... interact with synovial fibroblasts (SFs), T cells, B cells, and other inflammatory cells to promote the production of a variety of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines, such as TNF-alpha, IL-1beta, IL-8, and IL-6, which are involved in the pathogenic process of inflammatory arthritis.

evidence.reference_title: The Tumor-Like Phenotype of Rheumatoid Synovium: Molecular Profiling and Prospects for Precision Medicine.
evidence.snippet: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a systemic autoimmune disease characterized by destructive hyperplasia of the synovium. Fibroblast-like synoviocytes (FLS) are a major component of synovial pannus and actively participate in the pathologic progression of RA.
